assertions.text: V1 was traveling east on the exit ramp (implies motion east prior to departures).
V2 (tractor-trailer) was parked on the paved shoulder of the exit ramp facing east.
V1 first departed the roadway to the left, then returned to the roadway, then departed the roadway to the right before impact.
V1's front plane contacted the back plane of V2's trailer (V1 struck the rear of V2).
There was a single contact impact between V1 and V2 (one collision event described).
V2 was pushed forward as a result of the contact and came to rest near the point of impact.
After impact, V1 rotated clockwise and bounced back before coming to rest.
V1 came to rest on the left shoulder facing east.
The collision was sufficient to push the stationary tractor-trailer forward (more than a negligible tap).

verdicts.evidence: The vehicle was moving eastward prior to impact: the EDR pre-crash speed is [98.0 km/h at t=-4.75 s](T2) and [96.0 km/h at t=0.0 s](T2), indicating sustained motion before departure.
Telemetry is for V1 only and contains no sensor channel describing V2's parked status, orientation, or location relative to the shoulder; this is not present in the data and so is unverifiable.

The available channels (sparse pre-crash speed, steering, brake) do not provide lane-position or continuous lateral path; steering shows a peak of [40.5 deg at t=0.0 s](T2) but that alone cannot verify the sequence left-departure -> return -> right-departure, so the sequence is unverifiable from sensors.

IMU delta-V shows a large negative longitudinal change ([final cumulative delta-V -98.2 km/h](T1)) consistent with a frontal impact, but device-frame axes alignment to vehicle front/rear is undocumented; therefore direct claim that V1's front contacted V2's rear is unverifiable from these channels alone.

The crash-pulse shows a single dominant cumulative longitudinal delta-V reaching [−98.6 km/h at t=0.24 s](T2) and the delta-V samples form one primary pulse in the CISS window (EDR crash-pulse data in T1), consistent with a single contact event.

There is no telemetry from V2 in this dataset; V1's large negative delta-V ([final −98.2 km/h](T2)) shows a strong impact but whether V2 was pushed forward and its final rest position near impact cannot be determined from V1 sensors alone.

Steering peaked at [40.5 deg at t=0.0 s](T2) and there is high rotation-rate capability in channels, but the dataset lacks continuous yaw-rate or position traces to confirm a clockwise rotation and bounce-back; thus the post-impact rotation/bounce sequence is unverifiable.

Final rest position and heading (left shoulder facing east) are spatial facts not present in V1 telemetry channels; speed and delta-V ([final −98.2 km/h](T2)) do not give rest location or facing direction, so this is unverifiable.

The recorded longitudinal delta-V is large: the EDR summary reports a maximum delta-V of [−99.1 km/h](T1) and cumulative delta-V reaches [−98.6 km/h at t=0.24 s](T2), which indicates a substantial impact capable of moving a stationary large vehicle rather than a negligible tap.

result.rationale: V1's onboard telemetry (speed, steering, and crash-pulse) supports that V1 was traveling at high speed prior to a single severe impact with a large negative longitudinal delta-V, and that the impact was severe enough to likely move another vehicle. However, details about V2 (parked orientation, being pushed forward, rest positions) and the precise sequence of lane departures and post-impact rotation cannot be verified from V1-only channels.

result.time_zero: impact (t=0); pre-crash negative, crash pulse positive

channels.name: delta_v_long
channels.desc: Cumulative longitudinal delta-V during impact (times converted from ms to s)

result.notes: NHTSA CISS EDR data: sparse pre-crash series (~2 Hz, t<0) plus a crash-pulse delta-V curve (t>=0, ~10 ms steps). t=0 is the impact.

delta_v_long.note: cumulative velocity change during the crash pulse
